April 9, 2025 - by Pamela Langham

ABA’s 2024 Legal Technology Survey Report: Trends in Online Research
====================================================================

 The American Bar Association’s 2024 Legal Technology Survey Report, released on March 3, 2025, provides a comprehensive analysis of technology usage within the legal profession. Based on surveys conducted from October to December 2024, the report is divided into five volumes, examining trends in online research, marketing and communication technology, law office technology, litigation, and technology basics and security. This article focuses specifically on summarizing the key statistical findings related to trends in online research.

**Legal Research**

 Legal professionals dedicate a significant portion of their time to research, with an average of 19% of their work hours allocated to this essential task. This workload varies slightly depending on practice type. Solo practitioners spend the most time on research, at 23%, while those in firms of various sizes (2-9, 10-49, and 100 or more attorneys) report an average of 19%. Certain demographic groups and job roles also lean even more heavily on research. Attorneys admitted to the bar within the last decade devote the highest share, at 32%, while those specializing in employment or labor law dedicating the lowest time, at 24%.

**Research Tools**

 Participants in the survey were questioned about the frequency of their use of various formats for legal research. They indicated that paid online services are the format they use the most, with free online resources coming in second, followed by print materials, which are also used consistently. Not surprisingly, the larger the firm, the more likely its lawyers are to use paid online services. Conversely, smaller firms are more likely to use free online resources. However, legal print materials remain a favorite among lawyers, with 31% reporting they use print materials regularly. The most often used free online resources reportedly being used are government websites, FindLaw, Cornell’s Legal Information Institute, Google Scholar, and FastCase.

**Use of AI Tools**

 Participants were surveyed regarding their firm’s present or future adoption of generative artificial intelligence (AI) driven technology tools. The findings reveal that 30% are already utilizing such tools, a notable increase compared to 11% in 2023, 12% in 2022, and 10% in 2021. With firms of one hundred or more attorneys, 16% were using AI tools in 2023. In 2024, 46% of the larger firms are now employing AI tools - that’s almost a 35% increase. Firms of 10-49 attorneys increased their use of AI tools by 36%, and firms of 2-9 attorneys increased their use by almost 38%, in comparison to the preceding year. Solo attorneys increased their usage by 55.5%. Additionally, 15% of all law firms are seriously considering purchasing AI-based technology tools, while 15% are not interested, a slight decrease in 2023 from 21%. The data indicates a significant upward trend in the adoption of AI-driven tools among law firms.

**Benefits of AI Tools**

 The survey results on the perceived benefits of AI technology tools for law firms have changed significantly from prior years. The findings indicate a significant increase in the recognition of AI’s ability to save time and enhance efficiency, with 54% of respondents identifying this as the most important benefit, up from 44% in 2023 and 26% in 2022. Other benefits, such as document management and review, have seen modest growth. Additionally, the percentage of respondents uncertain about AI’s benefits has decreased, reflecting a growing familiarity and confidence in AI technology among legal professionals.

**Mainstreaming AI in Law**

 There were also shifts in perceptions about the mainstreaming of AI in the legal profession. Currently, 13% of respondents believe AI is already mainstream, a notable increase from previous years. Looking ahead, 45% anticipate AI will become mainstream within the next three years, with solo attorneys being the most optimistic at 53%. This trend is consistent across firms of various sizes, though slightly lower for larger firms. Additionally, 16% expect AI to be mainstream in 4-5 years, 6% in 6-10 years, and 2% in more than 10 years. Interestingly, uncertainty about AI’s future has decreased, with only 8% unsure when it will become mainstream and 9% admitting they lack sufficient knowledge about AI.

**Significant Concerns**

 Law firms revealed significant concerns about implementing and utilizing AI-based technology tools. The primary issues identified include the accuracy and reliability of the technology, data privacy and security, and the cost of implementation. Accuracy was a major concern for 75% of respondents, with higher percentages among firms with 10-49 attorneys (81%), firms with 2-9 attorneys and those with 100 or more attorneys (77% each), and solo attorneys (60%). Additionally, 1% of respondents were unaware of any major concerns, and another 1% had no concerns at all. Six percent of respondents felt they did not yet know enough about AI to provide an opinion, with this uncertainty being highest among solo attorneys (13%) and lower among larger firms.

**Conclusion**

 The ABA’s 2024 Legal Technology Survey Report regarding online research highlights the growing integration of technology in the legal profession. Many of the numbers regarding the amount and who is conducting online research are not surprising; however, the tools and methods are evolving. The report demonstrates that the adoption rates of AI are rising among firms of all sizes, indicating that legal professionals are increasingly recognizing the benefits of AI in saving time and improving efficiency. Despite valid concerns about accuracy, data privacy, and costs, the momentum towards embracing AI is undeniable. This suggests changes in the legal profession, where AI will likely become a fundamental aspect of practice management and legal research, reshaping how legal services are delivered in the years to come.

# [ABA Journal](https://www.abajournal.com/)

![ABA Journal](/images/resources/images-global/abajournal_logo_white.svg)

# The top legal technology trends of 2023

By [Nicole Black](https://www.abajournal.com/authors/64800/ "View this author's information")

December 21, 2023, 10:53 am CST

![bluesky share icon](/images/icons/bluesky-share.svg)
![](/images/icons/facebook_icon.svg)

![Nicole Black](https://www.abajournal.com/images/main_images/NicoleBlack2.jpg)

![Nicole Black](https://www.abajournal.com/images/main_images/NicoleBlack2.jpg)

*Nicole Black.*

The past year has been one defined by unpredictability and economic uncertainty as well as the lasting impact of the pandemic on technology adoption and the emergence of generative artificial intelligence. In the wake of these developments, lawyers, traditionally tech-resistant, began to embrace advanced tools even as there was a sudden and marked downshift in legal technology investments and mergers.

Additionally, while law firm leaders faced resistance to back-to-office demands, there were ongoing regulatory debates focused on nonlawyer participation in the practice of law. In other words, it was a year of seismic change, with no sign of stability in sight.

## Enter generative AI

In March, OpenAI released GPT-4, and generative AI tools rapidly gained prominence in the legal industry, for better or for worse. As experts predicted its significant impact early on, some lawyers jumped onto the generative AI bandwagon without fully understanding its benefits and drawbacks, making headlines in the process. Others took a more cautious approach, waiting for the technology to mature before integrating it into their workflows.

Maturity came surprisingly quickly, with leading legal tech firms like [LexisNexis](https://www.abajournal.com/web/article/lexisnexis-announces-new-generative-ai-platform-lexis-ai) and [Thomson Reuters](https://www.abajournal.com/web/article/thomson-reuters-partners-with-microsoft-for-generative-ai-push) integrating generative AI into their offerings during the second half of the year. Additionally, many other legal technology companies released beta generative AI tools, further popularizing this technology. In fact, according to the MyCase and LawPay 2024 Legal Industry Report, which will be released in January, nearly 27% of legal professionals reported using generative AI for work-related purposes, indicating a rapid adoption rate within the profession.

## The changing mindset of lawyers toward technology

In 2023, there was a noticeable shift in the mindset of legal professionals. While its direct health impacts remain uncertain, the pandemic’s influence in accelerating technology adoption has been undeniable. Lawyers and judges, traditionally viewed as tech-averse, are now embracing tools like Zoom, iPads and smartwatches with surprising enthusiasm.

Post-pandemic, as lawyers returned to offices—often in a hybrid model—they displayed a newfound curiosity about technology. This change in attitude came at an opportune moment, coinciding with the introduction of new generative AI technologies in the legal field even as legal technology funding declined.

## Legal Tech M&A and investments decline in 2023

In [last year’s roundup](https://www.abajournal.com/columns/article/top-4-legal-technology-news-stories-of-2022), I covered the high levels of technology investment and acquisitions occurring in the legal industry. After summarizing all of the M&A activity that occurred in 2022, I wondered whether this type of activity represented a new normal or whether we’d see a notable decrease in activity in the coming year.

Sitting squarely at the end of 2023, it’s clear that the previous boom in legal technology investments and acquisitions has tapered off. This year, there was a clear decrease in M&A activity due to the effects of the unstable economy, leading to reduced risk-taking and fewer legal technology investments.
But despite these broader market trends, there were still key strategic investments that stood out. The most notable was Thomson Reuters’ acquisition of Casetext and its generative AI product, CoCounsel, for a [whopping $650 million](https://www.abajournal.com/web/article/thomson-reuters-to-acquire-legal-tech-startup-casetext-for-650-million), highlighting the high level of interest in generative AI technology. While overall investment levels were down, moves like this indicate a strategic quality-over-quantity approach, with companies betting on cutting-edge technologies that promise to have a long-term impact on the practice of law.

## Hybrid work and navigating employee pushback

Another trend was the struggle of law firm leaders to [enforce back-to-work mandates](https://www.abajournal.com/magazine/article/are-law-firm-leaders-having-second-thoughts-on-remote-work). Post-pandemic, remote work has become more prevalent, leading to more flexible and innovative ways of working. Many lawyers and law firm employees prefer the remote work arrangement at least some of the time. As a result, despite the best efforts of employers locked into costly long-term commercial leases to lure employees back to physical offices, for many, the traditional five-day workweek has given way to hybrid schedules.

This shift in work culture has significant implications for the legal industry, particularly in terms of talent retention and recruitment. As prospective employees increasingly prioritize work-life balance and flexibility, law firms are forced to reconsider traditional work models to remain competitive. This trend could result in a permanent reconfiguration of law offices, which could dramatically impact the footprint of law firms.

## The ongoing battle over relaxing regulatory rules in the legal profession

In 2023, the legal industry [continued to grapple with key regulatory changes relating to the role of nonlawyers in legal services](https://www.abajournal.com/web/article/when-it-comes-to-deregulation-of-the-legal-industry-divisions-run-deep). In 2020, Utah established a [regulatory sandbox](https://www.abajournal.com/web/article/utah-embraces-nonlawyer-ownership-of-law-firms-as-part-of-broad-reforms), and Arizona [decided to eliminate Ethics Rule 5.4](https://www.abajournal.com/web/article/arizona-approves-alternative-business-structures-as-part-of-access-to-justice-reforms), which had the effect of allowing nonlawyer ownership in law firms. These reforms aimed to bridge the justice gap by ensuring legal assistance is available to those who need it most.

Despite the potential of these efforts, they sparked intense debates within the profession. Proponents argued that this type of reform would democratize legal services, increasing accessibility and affordability. Critics, however, raised concerns about a potential decline in the quality and integrity of legal services that could arise as a result of these efforts. As 2023 unfolded, these regulatory experiments became focal points for a broader discussion about the future of legal service delivery and the pressing need to address access to justice issues.

Looking back at 2023, it was notable that despite the economic uncertainty, the profession underwent significant change. Legal technology adoption increased, as did curiosity about emerging generative AI tools. A profession steeped in tradition pivoted and adapted to new norms, experimenting with hybrid work amid changing attitudes about technology adoption. As legal technology investment trends fluctuated throughout the year, the continuous regulatory discussions highlighted an evolution of mindset, setting the stage for continued technology adoption in the AI era.

# Legal Technology Trends That Will Define 2023

Date

December 2022

Category

Legal Tech

Author

Mike Ross | Chief Customer Officer

Share

It’s been a strong year for technology-driven legal teams; [63% posted profitability increases](https://www.wolterskluwer.com/en/expert-insights/legal-technology-trends-2022) in 2022 alone. According to the Wolters Kluwer Future Ready Lawyers survey, 87% of corporate lawyers consider technology as either “extremely” or “very” important to their work. 

In 2022, we’ve seen a number of trends shape up:

* Documents migrating to the cloud for faster searching, sharing, and tracking.
* Legal teams leveraging automation for e-discovery, research, and contract review.
* Artificial Intelligence (AI) assisting with predictive modeling and contract compliance.
* Virtual legal assistants handling low-level tasks, freeing staff to pursue value-rich work.

These same [trends](https://www.lexcheck.com/resources/top-legal-tech-trends-of-2022-lc) will carry on into the upcoming year, with several areas of unprecedented growth anticipated to boost legal efficiency, productivity, and performance. Let’s take a look at five exciting and upcoming legal technology trends for 2023.

## 1. Automated Contract Review Will Accelerate Deal Cycles

Review and negotiation comprise the lengthiest portions of the contracting process. A typically junior-level attorney must comb through every line of a new draft, compare it to the company’s preferred legal positions, and diligently apply best practices. Once this preliminary sweep is finished, senior legal counsel must then take one final look before it’s passed on to the other party—at which point a draft could bounce back and forth between the two several times. Manual review can take 30 days or more to complete, significantly delaying the entire sales cycle. Smart legal departments that capitalize on [contract automation](https://www.lexcheck.com/resources/contract-optimization-for-faster-agreements-and-better-results-lc) can complete the first pass in five minutes and pass entire deals within five days.

**Efficiency**: Manual contract review is often tedious and dull work that’s perfectly suited for AI.

**Productivity**: With automation, contracts can be reviewed in five minutes—not five days.

**Performance**: As a result, legal departments can handle larger volumes of contracts.

## 2. AI-Powered Tools Will Drive Negotiations Forward

[AI-powered contract redlining](https://www.lexcheck.com/resources/automated-contract-redlining-redefines-contract-negotiations-lc), risk analysis, and review tools can focus negotiations considerably. When an automated system detects an error or omission in a contract, for example, it can intelligently mark up the text and enable teams to direct workflows to the appropriate personnel. Wherever suggestions are made, the system inserts contextual notes to explain how and why a section should be improved. This, in turn, clarifies positions and eliminates unnecessary back-and-forths between parties during negotiations.

**Efficiency**: AI instantly finds standard clauses and boilerplate language to add contextual suggestions.

**Productivity**: Teams quickly see where to focus their attention during review and negotiation.

**Performance**: Trust and relationships improve as parties get on the same page faster.

## 3. Machine Learning Will Assure Standardization

Of all the legal technology trends, machine learning has the most potential to revolutionize workflows and standardization initiatives. Machine learning technology allows lawyers to mold systems to their exact specifications and preferences, automating updates to preferred positions that would otherwise be too time-consuming to find and correct manually. Reviewing two contracts side by side is a painstakingly slow process, whereas technology can check for compliance with a [Digital Playbook](https://www.lexcheck.com/ai-playbook) in mere minutes. With all that time saved, lawyers can focus on more pressing, strategic matters.

**Efficiency**: Lawyers can input custom “if/then” rules to easily automate draft changes.

**Productivity**: Technology automatically scans and compares documents, obviating laborious manual tasks.

**Performance**: Lawyers have more time to focus on strategic positioning and negotiation.

## 4. Automated Workflows Will Simplify Training

In the past, new employees had to read, digest, and nearly memorize entire corporate legal playbooks to understand the company’s lexicon and negotiating preferences. While clause libraries and templates helped, they still needed to know where to look to find answers to their questions. Today, teams that use Digital Playbooks and automated workflows lean on technology to do the heavy lifting for them. All the information they need to make key decisions is automatically extracted for them, with answers and the ability to make changes just a few clicks away.

**Efficiency**: Automation can reduce training and onboarding costs [by 99%](https://www.lexcheck.com/resources/save-time-and-money-on-legal-operations-training-with-automated-contract-review-lc).

**Productivity**: Technology can guide staff in handling compliance issues and corrections.

**Performance**: Technology preserves institutional knowledge, even after key staff members leave.

## 5. Data Analytics Will Improve Performance

Data analytics platforms are often used as part of an overarching contract management system, providing contract fulfillment tracking and valuable post-execution metrics. Teams can also look back at which clauses were most quickly approved and which ones languished in extensive back-and–forth negotiations. Come renegotiation time, contracts can be significantly improved. 

**Efficiency**: Important metrics are extracted and saved in auto-generated reports.

**Productivity**: Manual tracking of important deliverables and deadlines is no longer necessary.

**Performance**: Counsel can receive insights into which clauses are most and least heavily negotiated.

# What Are the Top Trends in Legal Technology Adoption This Year?

The legal industry is experiencing a significant transformation as **legal technology adoption** continues to accelerate. This year, several key trends are shaping how law firms and legal departments leverage technology to enhance their operations, improve client service, and stay competitive in an increasingly digital landscape. Understanding these trends is crucial for legal professionals looking to harness the power of technology in their practice.

One of the most prominent trends in legal technology adoption is the increasing use of **artificial intelligence** (AI) and machine learning in various aspects of legal work. AI-powered tools are revolutionizing tasks such as legal research, contract analysis, and due diligence. These technologies can quickly sift through vast amounts of data, identify relevant information, and even predict case outcomes with a high degree of accuracy. For example, AI-driven contract review platforms can analyze hundreds of contracts in a fraction of the time it would take a human lawyer, flagging potential issues and inconsistencies for further review.

The adoption of AI in legal practice goes beyond mere efficiency gains. It’s also changing the way lawyers approach their work. By automating routine tasks, AI frees up lawyers to focus on higher-value activities that require human judgment and creativity. This shift is leading to a reevaluation of legal workflows and the skills required for success in the modern legal profession. Law firms are increasingly seeking lawyers who are not only well-versed in the law but also comfortable with technology and data analysis.

Another significant trend in legal technology adoption is the widespread implementation of **cloud-based practice management systems**. These platforms offer a comprehensive suite of tools for managing all aspects of a law firm’s operations, from case management and time tracking to billing and client communications. The cloud-based nature of these systems allows for greater flexibility and accessibility, enabling lawyers to work remotely and collaborate more effectively with colleagues and clients.

The move to cloud-based systems is particularly relevant in the context of the ongoing shift towards remote and hybrid work models. Law firms that have embraced cloud technology have found themselves better equipped to adapt to the challenges posed by the global pandemic and the changing expectations of both clients and employees regarding work arrangements. This trend is likely to continue as firms recognize the benefits of cloud-based systems in terms of scalability, cost-effectiveness, and improved client service.

**[Cybersecurity](https://attorneys.media/cybercrime-attorney-digital-rights-protection/)** remains a top priority in legal technology adoption, with law firms increasingly recognizing the critical importance of protecting sensitive client data. As cyber threats become more sophisticated, law firms are investing in advanced security measures to safeguard their digital assets. This includes implementing multi-factor authentication, encryption technologies, and regular security audits. Additionally, many firms are adopting comprehensive cybersecurity training programs for their staff to ensure that human error doesn’t compromise their digital defenses.

The focus on cybersecurity is not just about protecting against external threats. It’s also about maintaining client trust and complying with increasingly stringent data protection regulations. Law firms are finding that robust cybersecurity measures are becoming a key differentiator in attracting and retaining clients, particularly in industries that handle sensitive information.

**Blockchain technology** is emerging as a significant trend in legal technology adoption, particularly in areas such as intellectual property, real estate, and contract management. Blockchain’s ability to create immutable, transparent records is proving valuable in verifying ownership, tracking changes, and ensuring the integrity of legal documents. Smart contracts, which are self-executing contracts with the terms of the agreement directly written into code, are gaining traction as a way to automate and streamline certain legal processes.

The adoption of blockchain in legal practice is still in its early stages, but its potential to revolutionize certain aspects of legal work is significant. For example, blockchain-based systems for intellectual property registration could provide a more efficient and secure way to establish and track ownership rights. In real estate, blockchain could streamline property transfers and reduce the need for intermediaries in transactions.

**Legal analytics** tools are becoming increasingly sophisticated and widely adopted. These platforms use data analysis and machine learning to provide insights into legal strategies, judge behavior, and case outcomes. By analyzing vast amounts of historical case data, these tools can help lawyers make more informed decisions about litigation strategies, settlement negotiations, and case resource allocation.

The use of legal analytics is changing the way lawyers approach case preparation and client advice. Instead of relying solely on personal experience and intuition, lawyers can now back up their strategies with data-driven insights. This trend is particularly evident in areas such as patent litigation, where the ability to analyze large datasets of past cases can provide a significant strategic advantage.

**[Virtual and augmented reality](https://attorneys.media/legal-tech-revolutionizing-legal-services/)** technologies are beginning to find applications in the legal field, particularly in areas such as courtroom presentation and client communication. These immersive technologies can be used to create compelling visual representations of crime scenes, accident reconstructions, or complex technical processes, helping juries and judges better understand the facts of a case.

While still in the early stages of adoption, VR and AR have the potential to significantly enhance the way legal arguments are presented and understood. As these technologies become more accessible and user-friendly, we can expect to see their use expand in legal education, client consultations, and even remote court proceedings.

The trend towards **legal process automation** continues to gain momentum, with law firms and legal departments looking to streamline routine tasks and workflows. This includes the use of document automation tools for creating standardized legal documents, chatbots for handling initial client inquiries, and workflow automation platforms for managing complex legal processes.

Legal process automation is not just about efficiency; it’s also about improving accuracy and consistency in legal work. By reducing the potential for human error in routine tasks, automation can help law firms minimize risks and improve the overall quality of their services. This trend is particularly relevant for high-volume, repetitive legal tasks such as contract generation, due diligence, and compliance monitoring.

**E-discovery** tools are becoming more sophisticated and integral to litigation practice. Advanced e-discovery platforms now incorporate AI and machine learning capabilities to more effectively identify relevant documents, recognize patterns, and even predict which documents are likely to be privileged. This evolution in e-discovery technology is helping law firms manage the ever-increasing volume of electronic data involved in modern litigation more efficiently and cost-effectively.

The adoption of advanced e-discovery tools is also changing the skill set required for litigation support roles. Legal professionals involved in e-discovery now need to be proficient in data analysis, project management, and technology implementation, in addition to their legal knowledge.

**[Legal research platforms](https://attorneys.media/are-online-legal-consultations-confidential/)** are evolving to incorporate more advanced search capabilities, natural language processing, and predictive analytics. These enhancements are making legal research more efficient and comprehensive, allowing lawyers to quickly find relevant cases, statutes, and legal analyses. Some platforms are even beginning to offer features that can suggest relevant legal arguments or precedents based on the facts of a case.

The improvement in legal research technology is not just about speed and efficiency; it’s also about expanding the depth and breadth of legal analysis. By providing access to a wider range of legal materials and offering sophisticated analytical tools, these platforms are enabling lawyers to develop more nuanced and well-supported legal arguments.

**[Client portals](https://attorneys.media/are-online-legal-consultations-confidential/)** and collaboration tools are becoming standard offerings for many law firms. These secure online platforms allow clients to access case information, documents, and billing details in real-time. They also facilitate easier communication and collaboration between lawyers and clients, improving transparency and client satisfaction.

The adoption of client portals reflects a broader trend towards greater transparency and client-centricity in legal services. By providing clients with direct access to information about their cases and facilitating easier communication, law firms are responding to growing client expectations for more responsive and accessible legal services.

**[Alternative legal service providers](https://attorneys.media/legal-tech-revolutionizing-legal-services/)** (ALSPs) are leveraging technology to disrupt traditional legal service delivery models. These providers, which include legal process outsourcing companies, managed services providers, and legal tech startups, are using technology to offer more efficient and cost-effective solutions for certain types of legal work.

The rise of ALSPs is challenging traditional law firms to innovate and adapt their own service models. Many law firms are responding by developing their own technology-enabled service offerings or partnering with ALSPs to provide more comprehensive solutions to their clients.

**Data privacy and compliance** tools are becoming increasingly important as data protection regulations like GDPR and CCPA continue to evolve. Law firms are adopting specialized software to help them and their clients navigate the complex landscape of data privacy regulations. These tools can help automate compliance processes, manage consent, and track data flows to ensure adherence to relevant regulations.

The focus on data privacy technology reflects the growing importance of data protection in legal practice. As data breaches and privacy violations become more costly and damaging to reputation, law firms are recognizing the need to not only advise clients on data privacy issues but also to ensure their own practices are compliant and secure.

**Legal project management** (LPM) software is gaining traction as law firms seek to improve efficiency and predictability in legal service delivery. These tools help lawyers plan, execute, and monitor legal matters more effectively, providing better visibility into project progress, resource allocation, and budget management.

The adoption of LPM software is part of a broader trend towards more business-like approaches to legal practice. By applying project management principles and tools to legal work, firms can improve profitability, enhance client satisfaction, and better manage the complexities of modern legal matters.

**Predictive coding** in document review is becoming more sophisticated and widely adopted. This technology uses machine learning algorithms to identify relevant documents in large datasets, significantly reducing the time and cost associated with document review in litigation and due diligence processes.

The evolution of predictive coding technology is not only improving efficiency but also changing the nature of document review work. Lawyers are increasingly taking on more supervisory roles in the review process, focusing on quality control and strategic decision-making rather than manual document review.

**Legal chatbots** and virtual assistants are being deployed by law firms and legal departments to handle routine client inquiries, schedule appointments, and even provide basic legal information. While these AI-powered tools are not replacing human lawyers, they are helping to improve client service by providing quick responses to common questions and freeing up lawyers to focus on more complex tasks.

The adoption of legal chatbots reflects a broader trend towards the use of conversational AI in customer service across various industries. In the legal context, these tools are particularly useful for handling initial client intake, providing general legal information, and triaging inquiries to the appropriate legal professionals.

**Regulatory technology** (RegTech) solutions are becoming increasingly important for law firms and their clients. These tools help organizations stay compliant with complex and ever-changing regulations by automating compliance processes, monitoring regulatory changes, and providing real-time alerts and reporting.

The adoption of RegTech solutions is particularly relevant in highly regulated industries such as finance, healthcare, and energy. Law firms that can effectively leverage these technologies are better positioned to provide comprehensive compliance advice and services to their clients.

In conclusion, the legal technology landscape is rapidly evolving, with new tools and platforms emerging to address various aspects of legal practice. From AI-powered legal research to blockchain-based contract management, these technologies are reshaping how legal services are delivered and consumed. As adoption continues to accelerate, law firms and legal departments that embrace these trends will be better positioned to meet the changing needs of clients, improve operational efficiency, and maintain a competitive edge in an increasingly technology-driven legal market.

Staying Ahead of the Curve: 6 Top Legal Tech Trends to Watch in 2023
====================================================================

Written on August 7, 2023.

![Image 3](https://magazine.arma.org/wp-content/themes/yootheme/cache/M1267_cover-a0695c76.png)

The legal industry has earned its cautious reputation to “watch and wait” before adopting technology. But forward-looking organizations are increasingly turning to technology to automate processes, streamline operations, enhance customer service and collaboration, increase efficiency, and remain competitive. As technology continues to evolve and become more accessible, law firms need to stay on top of the latest legal tech advancements.

In 2023, we see six significant trends that considerably impact the legal industry. These include artificial intelligence (AI), hybrid and remote work collaboration, cloud, workflow automation and customization, mail processing and digitization, and security and data privacy.

**Trend #1: The Rise of AI**
----------------------------

Incorporating Artificial Intelligence (AI) technology into legal practice is no longer a “new” frontier. It’s become accessible in our day-to-day lives—with offerings from companies such as Microsoft, Nvidia, and OpenAI. While we are still a far cry from AI lawyers replacing human lawyers, AI could revolutionize the legal industry by automating mundane tasks such as legal research, document review, and contract analysis. It’s a tool to help lawyers do what they do every day but do their jobs more efficiently, such as by quickly and accurately reviewing large amounts of data or automatically classifying data based on content and context.

AI can be a valuable tool. But it’s important to ensure that AI tools are utilized responsibly and ethically and that good governance is employed. And this means establishing clear user policies and being mindful of the risks and benefits associated with these AI tools.

**Trend #2: Hybrid and Remote Work**
------------------------------------

If the pandemic taught us one thing, it was that moving from a 100% office setup to an entirely virtual workspace almost overnight was possible. Switching to a hybrid or fully remote setup has several advantages for law firms and their employees. For one, it eliminates the need for a chunk of office space—one of the most significant expenses for firms. Another advantage comes in wooing talent, which increasingly demands flexible remote work options.

With hybrid and remote work settings, we see an upsurge in collaboration between co-workers and clients. To facilitate communication and collaboration, firms are investing in technology such as Slack, Zoom, Asana, and Dropbox to accommodate this “new normal.”

With so much work between colleagues and clients shifting online comes a need for strong Information governance policies to guarantee data security and compliance. What third-party and cloud-based solutions are being used by your employees? Are their levels of security and privacy protocols leaving them vulnerable to breaches and cyberattacks?

Implementing a comprehensive security and data governance strategy is critical to safeguard information and sensitive data. This includes measures such as encryption, two-factor authentication, and regular security audits. Moreover, you need policies and procedures to ensure all employees know their data security and privacy responsibilities and provide training on those protocols. And lastly, have a plan of action to respond to any security incidents or data breaches.

**Trend #3: Cloud Flexibility**
-------------------------------

Law firms are moving to the cloud in droves. It’s easy to understand why. Cloud-based solutions provide greater flexibility and scalability. Firms can access data from any location at any time, which improves collaboration with clients and colleagues. Cloud-based solutions are being used to store and share documents, as well as to access legal research. Moreover, cloud-based systems are more cost-effective than the alternative of buying servers, maintaining IT, and hiring more staff to keep everything running.

Many of the same best practices outlined for hybrid and remote work apply to cloud-based solutions. It’s important to establish data security policies and to ensure they are protected from unauthorized access. Tech-savvy law firms have a reliable backup and recovery system in place to ensure operational continuity, as well as a plan for migrating data to the cloud and implementing cloud-based solutions, with all stakeholders being aware of implementation timelines and guidelines.

**Trend #4:****Workflow Automation and Customization**
------------------------------------------------------

Workflow automation and customization is another trend that can help lawyers and law firms streamline their processes and improve efficiency. The process uses software to automate and customize legal functions such as document generation and management, time tracking, client communication, and more. Many law firms now have workflow automation and customization software and processes in place.

Before beginning workflow automation and customization processes, first, establish the goals you want to accomplish. What are your firm’s particular needs and budget? Research different workflow automation and customization software options to find one that meets your requirements and ensures the software is secure and reliable. Make sure to properly train staff on these applications and processes to get the most out of your investment.

**Trend #5: Mail Processing and Digitization**
----------------------------------------------

Digitizing, electronically filing, and applying information governance principles to documents is a trend rapidly gaining traction in the legal industry. This process allows faster document processing, reduces costs associated with manual document processing, and improves accuracy and security.

Law firms should use secure document scanning systems so that all documents are correctly scanned and stored. Optical Character Recognition (OCR) or text recognition technology can help extract data from scanned documents and ensure documents are correctly indexed and stored in a secure digital repository.

And make sure your new system is compatible with existing systems and processes. When your system is up and running, regularly audit and monitor it to ensure all documents are appropriately scanned and stored.

**Trend #6: Security and Data Privacy**
---------------------------------------

Finally, one of the most pressing trends is security and data privacy. Law firms must prioritize data security and privacy given the sensitive nature of the data they manage and the need to protect their and their clients’ confidential data.

Lawyers must remain aware of the various security and data privacy regulations that can affect their operations, such as the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) in the European Union, the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) in California, and the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) in the United States.

Staying abreast of these laws ensures your office remains compliant. The penalties for violating the GDPR, CCPA, and HIPAA vary depending on the severity of the violation. For GDPR violations, organizations can be fined [up to 4% of their global annual turnover or €20 million](https://www.eqs.com/compliance-blog/biggest-gdpr-fines/) (whichever is greater). For CCPA violations, the fine is [up to $7,500 per violation](https://www.techtarget.com/searchsecurity/feature/10-CCPA-enforcement-cases-from-the-laws-first-year). For HIPAA violations, fines can be [up to $1.5 million per violation](https://www.hipaajournal.com/what-are-the-penalties-for-hipaa-violations-7096/).

A comprehensive data security policy outlining the procedures for handling and protecting sensitive data is imperative for every law firm. Ensure employees are trained on the best data security practices and regularly review and update your security measures.

By staying abreast of the latest technological trends, you can remain competitive and provide clients with the most up-to-date services that serve them and your business well. And you’ll remain at the forefront of the industry.

enriched_points.expanded_content: AI tools are being widely adopted across law firms, facilitating tasks from document review to legal research, enhancing efficiency and productivity significantly.
Clients now demand more transparency and accessibility, prompting firms to leverage technology for better communication and service delivery, ultimately driving client satisfaction.
The hybrid work model has transformed legal practices, enabling enhanced collaboration through digital tools, while maintaining flexibility for attorneys and clients alike.
The legal tech landscape is poised for continuous growth, with investments in AI, cloud solutions, and cybersecurity to meet evolving regulatory and client needs.
